title: LADR
text:
Website for learning Linear Algebra Done Right (LADR) 1. The book is open access and LADR4e.pdf is available.
Custom callouts are used on this website 2. The custom.scss must be referred in the YAML.

title: LADR
text:
Footnotes


Axler,S. “Linear Algebra Done Right”, 4th Edition, Springer, 2024↩︎
youtube: How to create CUSTOM Callout Boxes in Quarto.↩︎

title: 1A-RC
text:
inverse, subtraction and division in \(\mathbb{C}\)


1.1 definition: -\(\alpha\), subtraction, 1/\(\alpha\) division


Suppose \(\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}\)

Let \(-\alpha\) denote the additive inverse of \(\alpha\). Thus \(-\alpha\) ia a unique complex number such that: \[\alpha + (- \alpha) = 0\]
Subtraction on \(\mathbb{C}\) is defined by: \[\beta - \alpha = \beta + (- \alpha)\]
For \(\alpha \ne 0\), let 1/\(\alpha\) and \(\frac{1}{\alpha}\) denote the multiplicative inverse of \(\alpha\). Thus 1/\(\alpha\) is a unique complex number such that: \[ \alpha(1/\alpha) = 1 \]
For \(\alpha \ne 0\), division by \(\alpha\) is defined by: \[ \beta/\alpha = \beta(1/\alpha) \]





n-dimensional \(\mathbb{F}\)


1.11 definition: \(\mathbb{F}^n\), coordinate


\(\mathbb{F}^n\) is a set of all lists of length \(n\) elements of \(\mathbb{F}\):
\[ \mathbb{F}^n = \{(x_1, \ldots, x_n): x_k \in \mathbb{F} \text{ for } k = 1,\ldots,n\}.\]
For \((x_1, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbb{F}^n\) and \(k \in (1,\ldots,n)\) we say that \(x_k\) is the \(k^{th}\) coordinate of \((x_1,\ldots,x_n)\).




Addition in \(\mathbb{F}^n\)


1.13 definition: addition in \(\mathbb{F}^n\)


Addition in \(\mathbb{F}^n\) is defined by adding the corresponding coordinates: \[
(x_1,\ldots,x_n) + (y_1,\ldots,y_n)=(x_1+y_1,\ldots, x_n+y_n)
\]




1.15 notation: 0


Let \(O\) denote the list of length \(n\) whose coordinates are all \(0\): \[
O = (0,\ldots,0)
\]




1.17 additive inverse in \(\mathbb{F}^n\), -\(x\)


For \(x \in \mathbb{F}^n\), the additive inverse of \(x\), denoted by \(-x\), is the vector \(-x \in \mathbb{F}^n\) such that \[
x + (-x) = 0
\] Thus if \(x = x_1,\ldots,x_n\), then \(-x = (-x_1, \dots, -x_n )\).




1.18 definition: scalar multiplication in \(\mathbb{F}^n\)


The product of a number \(\lambda\) and a vector in \(\mathbb{F}^n\) is computed by multiplying each coordinate of the vector by \(\lambda\): \[
\lambda(x_1,\ldots,x_n) = (\lambda x_1, \ldots, \lambda x_n);
\] here \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\) and \((x_1,\ldots,x_n) \in \mathbb{F}^n\)

title: 1BVectorSpaces
text:
Vector Addition and Scalar Multiplication


1.19 definition: addition, scalar multiplication



An addition on a set V is a function that assigns and element \(u + v \in V\) to each pair of elements \(u,v \in V\)
A scalar multiple on a set V is a function that assigns an element \(\lambda v \in V\) to each \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\) and each \(v \in V\).





note:


Defn 1.19 ensures closure in \(V\)




Vectors


definition: vector


Elements of a vector space are called vectors or points.




note:



\(\mathbb{F}^n\) is a vector space over \(\mathbb{F}\) (with properties satisfying the definition of vector space given below).
The point or vector of \(\mathbb{F}^n\) is given by: \((x_1,\dots,x_n)\) where \(x_k \in \mathbb{F} \text{ for } k = 1,..,n\)





Vector Space


1.20 definition: vector space


A vector space is a set \(V\) along with an addition and scalar multiplication on \(V\) such that the following properties hold.
1. commutativity
\(u+v = v+ u\) for all \(u,v \in V\)
2. associativity
\((u+v)+w = v+(u+w)\) and \((ab)v = a(bv)\) for all \(u,v,w \in V\) and for all \(a,b \in F\).
3. additive identity
There exists an element \(0 \in V\) such that \(v + 0 = v\) for all \(v \in V\).
4. additive inverse
For every \(v \in V\) there exists \(w \in V\) such that \(v+w=0\).
5. multiplicative identity
\(1v = v\) for all \(v \in V\)
6. distributive properties
\(a(u+v) = au+av\) and \((a+b)v = av+bv\) for all \(a,b \in F\) and \(u,v \in V\).


A key difference between field and vector space is the multiplication by a scalar in the case of vectors. “Multiplication” between vectors is not defined. (thus no multiplicative inverse).
Examples: Vector spaces:
\(\mathbb{R}\) - real vector space over \(\mathbb{R}\)
\(\mathbb{C}\) - complex vector space over \(\mathbb{C}\)
\(\mathbb{F}^n\) - finite dimensional vector space over \(\mathbb{F}\)
\(\mathbb{F}^\infty\) - infinite vector space over \(\mathbb{F}\)
\(\mathbb{F}^S\) - the set of functions from \(S\) to \(F\)


Set of functions \(\mathbb{F}^S\) is a vector space.


notation: \(\mathbb{F}^S\)



If \(S\) is a set, then \(F^S\) denotes the set of functions from \(S\) to \(\mathbb{F}\).
For \(f,g \in \mathbb{F^S}\), the sum \(f+g \in \mathbb{F^S}\) is the function defined by \[(f+g)(x) = f(x)+g(x)\] for all \(x \in S\).
For \(\lambda \in \mathbb{F}\) and \(f\) in \(\mathbb{F^S}\), the product \(\lambda f \in \mathbb{F^S}\) is the function defined by \[ (\lambda f)(x) = \lambda f(x)\] for all \(x \in S.\)





Additive Identity and Inverse for \(\mathbb{F}^S\)


further notation: \(\mathbb{F}^S\)



the additive identity of \(\mathbb{F}^S\) is the function \(0 : \mathbb{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{F}\) defined by \[
0(x) = 0
\] for all \(x \in S\)
For \(f \in \mathbb{F}^S\), the additive inverse of \(f\) is the function \(-f : \mathbb{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{F}\) defined by \[
(-f)(x) = -f(x)
\] for all \(x \in S\)

title: 1CSubspaces
text:
1.20 definition: subspace


A subset \(U\) of \(V\) is called a subspace of \(V\) if \(U\) is also a vector space with the same additive identity, addition and scalar multiplication as on \(V\).




1.34 conditions for a subspace


A subset \(U\) of \(V\) is a subspace of \(V\) if and only if \(U\) satisfies the following three conditions.
additive identity
\(0 \in U\)
closed under addition
\(u,w \in U\) implies \(u+w \in U\)
closed under scalar multiplication
\(a \in F\) and \(u \in U\) implies \(au \in U\)


Examples: subspaces

The set of continuous real valued functions on the interval [0,1] is a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^{[0,1]}\)
the set of differentiable real-valued functions on \(\mathbb{R}\) is a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^{R}\)
the set of all sequences of complex numbers with limit 0 is a subspace of \(\mathbb{C}^\infty\).


Sums of Subspaces


1.20 definition: sum of subspace


Suppose \(V_1,\ldots,V_m\) are subspaces of \(V\). The sum of \(V_1,\ldots,V_m\), denoted by \(V_1+\ldots+V_m\), is the set of all possible sums of elements of \(V_1,\ldots,V_m\). More precisely, \[
V_1+\ldots,+V_m = \{v_1+\cdots+v_m : v_1 \in V,\ldots,v_m \in V_m\}
\]


Examples of sum of subspaces

a sum of subspaces of \(\mathbb{F}^3\)

If U is the set of elements of \(\mathbb{F}^3\) whose second and third coordinates equal 0, and W is the set of elements of \(\mathbb{F}^3\) whose first and third coordinates equal 0: \[
U = \{(x,0,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3: x \in \mathbb{F}\} \text{ and }
W = \{(0,y,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3: y \in \mathbb{F}\}
\] then, \[
U + W = \{(x,y,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3) : x,y \in \mathbb{F}\}
\]


Direct sums


1.20 definition: direct sum, \(\oplus\)


Suppose \(V_1,\ldots,V_m\) are subspaces of \(V\).

The sum of \(V_1+\cdots+V_m\), is called a direct sum if each element \(V_1+\cdots+V_m\) can be written in only one way as a sum \(v_1+\cdots+v_m\), where each \(v_k \in V_k\).
If \(V_1+\cdots+V_m\) is a direct sum, then \(V_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus V_m\) denotes \(V_1+\cdots+V_m\), with the \(\oplus\) notation serving as an indication that this is a direct sum.



Examples of direct sum \(\oplus\)

a sum of subspaces of \(\mathbb{F}^3\)

If U is ths subspace of \(\mathbb{F}^3\) whose second and third coordinates equal 0, and W is the set of elements of \(\mathbb{F}^3\) whose first and third coordinates equal 0: \[
U = \{(x,0,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3: x \in \mathbb{F}\} \text{ and }
W = \{(0,y,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3: y \in \mathbb{F}\}
\] then, \[
U + W = \{(x,y,0) \in \mathbb{F}^3) : x,y \in \mathbb{F}\}
\]

title: 2ASpan
text:
2.4 definition: span


The set of all linear combinations of a list of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) in \(V\) is call the span of \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\), denoted by span(\(v_1, \ldots, v_m\)). In other workds \[
span(v_1, \ldots, v_m) = (av_1+\ldots+a_m v_m : a_1,\ldots,a_m \in \mathbb{F})
\] The span of the empty list () is defined to be (0).





2.4 definition: polynomial, \(\mathbb{P(F)}\)


The set of all linear combinations of a list of vectors \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\) in \(V\) is call the span of \(v_1, \ldots, v_m\), denoted by span(\(v_1, \ldots, v_m\)). In other workds \[
span(v_1, \ldots, v_m) = (av_1+\ldots+a_m v_m : a_1,\ldots,a_m \in \mathbb{F})
\] The span of the empty list () is defined to be (0).
